figures.caption: TABLE I. Transformer encoder. Details on the encoder architecture used for the VAE. B refers to the batch size and I the input dimension. For the Rydberg experiment I = N , where N is the number of sites of the quantum system, while for the Cluster Ising model and the FKM I = 2N . For the two latter, we set the model dimension to dmodel = 16 and the number of heads to Nhead = 2, while for the former dmodel = 8 and Nhead = 4. The latent space dimension was chosen to be 5 for all cases.

TABLE II. Transformer decoder. Details on the decoder architecture. See Table I for definitions. As for the encoder, we consider two different architectures: one for the Rydberg experiment (I = N and dmodel = 8) and a slight variation for the Cluster Ising model and the FKM (I = 2N and dmodel = 32). Every transformer layer performs cross-attention to the latent variables z, embeddeded to dimension B × I × dmodel by means of a linear layer.

FIG. 1. Schematic representation of the proposed pipeline. a) An experimental setup produces data x of a quantum system for different values of the k experimental parameters θ1,...,k. b) A variational autoencoder (VAE) is trained on an ensemble of unlabeled quantum data collected from the previous experiment. It learns to reconstruct the conditional probabilities of its input and encodes the data in few latent variable z. Inspecting the values of the learned latent neurons across the parameter space will provide insight into the system’s phase space and can reveal unknown features. c) An unknown cluster appearing in the latent representation can be further explained and investigated using symbolic regression methods (SR). The latter seek for a compact analytic formula to discover an order parameter. Its structure can also provide insight into the nature of the cluster and the physics of the system.

FIG. 4. Hybrid data of the Falicov-Kimball model. a) Phase diagram of the FKM, based on [40, 41]. b) The two fermion species are measured, leading to a binary outcome (occupied or empty) for f fermions and a continuous local density for d fermions. c) Training of the VAE seen through the variances σ2 i of the latent variables output by the encoder. d) Latent representation learned by the VAE seen through the average of the absolute mean |µ1,2| across the parameter space. e) Conditional probabilities generated by the decoder of the VAE at two different locations in the ordered phase. f) The repulsion of f–d particles across the parameter space is seen through the product of the conditional probabilities of each species.

FIG. 7. Cluster Ising model: additional results. a) Average absolute values of three active latent variables across the parameter space. b) Longitudinal Z magnetization across the parameter space. c) Dataset used for the symbolic regression (SR). d) Prediction of the two-body correlator obtained when restricting the input to Z-basis measurements. e) Exponent η extracted from a polynomial fit of the X-bubbles distribution P (s) ∼ sη.

FIG. 5. Rydberg atoms: KL regularizations. Representations learned by training the VAE using a) the analytical KL regularization employed in the main text and b) the total correlation (TC) decomposition of the KL divergence, as used in [13].

TABLE V. Robustness of SR1. Performance of the SR classification scheme (SR1) in identifying the Néel in the presence of a noise in the ’in’ and ’out’ labels in its dataset. For each case, we report the Spearman correlation between the learned symbolic function f(x) and the corresponding order parameter as well as the classification AUC scores.

TABLE III. Convolutional encoder. Details on the encoder architecture used for the J1J2 model. B is the batch size, L = √ N the size of the square lattice and ks the kernel size. The latent space dimension was chosen to be 5.

TABLE IV. Comparison of symbolic methods. Performance of the symbolic regression methods (SR) in identifying the Néel, striped, and polarized phases. For each task, we report the Spearman correlation between the learned symbolic function f(x) and the corresponding order parameter as well as the classification AUC scores.

FIG. 6. J1J2 model. a) Phase diagram visualized using the next–nearest-neighbor (NNN) correlator. b) Training of the VAE seen through the log of the variances σ output by the encoder. c) Latent representation learned by the VAE. d) Classification objective of the first symbolic regression approach (SR1). e) Gradient alignment objective of the third symbolic regression methods (SR3). f) Predictions of the symbolic function f(x) obtained after training with the various SR objectives to characterize the Néel phase. g) Learned two-body correlator weights αij of f(x). Links are colored according to the sign of αij , orange being positive and purple negative. Their thickness scales with |αij |. Results with an additional L1 regularization are presented in the bottom rows. h) Prediction f(x) and its two-body correlator weights αij learned with SR1 for different classification datasets. Results are presented with the additional L1 regularization.
